county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Pittsburg County formed 1907 at statehood from the Choctaw Nation, Indian Territory. County seat is McAlester. Major coal mining center. Named for Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (original spelling variation). Home to the Oklahoma State Penitentiary.
county_metadata.research_tips: Pittsburg County was part of the Choctaw Nation in Indian Territory. McAlester, the county seat, was founded by J.J. McAlester (a Choctaw citizen) and became a major coal mining center. The Oklahoma State Penitentiary is in McAlester. Choctaw Nation tribal records (Dawes Rolls, allotment records) at NARA Fort Worth and the Choctaw Nation headquarters are essential. Coal mining records (employment, payroll, accident reports, union records) document diverse immigrant communities (Italian, Welsh, Lithuanian, Slovak). The Oklahoma Historical Society holds Indian Territory newspapers and court records. County Clerk records begin at statehood (1907). The Pittsburg County Genealogical and Historical Society has published cemetery transcriptions and family files. FamilySearch has Oklahoma marriage and probate records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Tribal allotment records (Dawes Act) at NARA Fort Worth for pre-statehood land in Pittsburg County; County Clerk deed books from 1907

source_profiles.survival_notes: County records complete from 1907; allotment records at NARA are substantially complete

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Pontotoc County formed 1907 at statehood from the Chickasaw Nation, Indian Territory. County seat is Ada, current headquarters of the Chickasaw Nation. Oil-producing county.
county_metadata.research_tips: Pontotoc County was part of the Chickasaw Nation in Indian Territory. Ada, the county seat, is now the headquarters of the Chickasaw Nation of Oklahoma. Chickasaw Nation tribal records (Dawes Rolls, allotment records, citizenship files) are extensively available at the Chickasaw Nation in Ada and NARA Fort Worth. Oil production in the county generated lease and royalty records. East Central University in Ada has regional history collections. County Clerk records begin at statehood (1907). The Oklahoma Historical Society holds Indian Territory newspapers and court records. The Pontotoc County Historical and Genealogical Society has local materials. FamilySearch has Oklahoma marriage and probate records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Tribal allotment records (Dawes Act) at NARA Fort Worth for pre-statehood land in Pontotoc County; County Clerk deed books from 1907

county_metadata.parent_county: Original county, Oklahoma Territory (Iowa/Sac & Fox/Potawatomi opening)
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Pottawatomie County formed 1891 from the Iowa/Sac & Fox/Potawatomi opening. County seat is Shawnee. Home to Citizen Potawatomi Nation, Absentee Shawnee Tribe, and Kickapoo Tribe of Oklahoma.
county_metadata.research_tips: Pottawatomie County was organized in 1891 after the Iowa, Sac and Fox, and Citizen Potawatomi lands were opened. Shawnee, the county seat, reflects the Absentee Shawnee tribal presence. Multiple tribal communities are in the county: Citizen Potawatomi Nation (headquarters in Shawnee), Absentee Shawnee Tribe (in Shawnee), and Kickapoo Tribe of Oklahoma. Tribal enrollment records at respective tribal headquarters and NARA Fort Worth are essential. Oil production was significant. County Clerk records begin from 1891. BLM GLO land patents document non-Indian settler claims. The Oklahoma Historical Society holds territorial newspapers and census records. The Mabee-Gerrer Museum in Shawnee and Oklahoma Baptist University have local materials. FamilySearch has Oklahoma marriage records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO federal land patents for Pottawatomie County; County Clerk deed books from 1891

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Pushmataha County formed 1907 at statehood from the Choctaw Nation, Indian Territory. County seat is Antlers. Named for Pushmataha, a Choctaw chief and US Army brigadier general.
county_metadata.research_tips: Pushmataha County was part of the Choctaw Nation in Indian Territory. The county is named for Pushmataha, a famous Choctaw chief. Pre-statehood records center on Choctaw Nation tribal records: Dawes Rolls (Choctaw by Blood, Choctaw Freedmen), allotment records, and tribal court records at NARA Fort Worth and the Choctaw Nation headquarters. The county was heavily timbered with lumber industry records. County Clerk records in Antlers begin at statehood (1907). The Oklahoma Historical Society holds Indian Territory newspapers and court records. Pushmataha County is one of the most rural and least populated in Oklahoma, with limited local resources. FamilySearch has Oklahoma marriage and probate records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Roger Mills County formed 1892 from former Cheyenne and Arapaho lands. County seat is Cheyenne. Named for Roger Q. Mills. Site of the Battle of the Washita (1868). Beckham and Ellis counties were later carved from it.
county_metadata.research_tips: Roger Mills County was organized in 1892 from former Cheyenne and Arapaho tribal lands. The Battle of the Washita (1868, Custer's attack on a Cheyenne village) took place in the county. County Clerk records in Cheyenne (the town) begin from 1892. NARA Fort Worth holds Cheyenne-Arapaho Agency records including allotment files and annuity rolls. BLM GLO land patents document settler claims. Note that Beckham and Ellis counties were later carved from Roger Mills -- for records after 1907, check the correct successor county. The Washita Battlefield National Historic Site has historical materials. The Oklahoma Historical Society holds territorial newspapers. FamilySearch has Oklahoma territorial and state marriage records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO federal land patents for Roger Mills County; County Clerk deed books from 1892

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Rogers County formed 1907 at statehood from the Cherokee Nation, Indian Territory. County seat is Claremore, hometown of Will Rogers. Named for Clement Vann Rogers, Will Rogers' father and Cherokee Nation senator.
county_metadata.research_tips: Rogers County was part of the Cherokee Nation in Indian Territory. Claremore, the county seat, is famous as the hometown of Will Rogers (born 1879). Cherokee Nation enrollment records, Dawes Rolls, and allotment records are critical for pre-statehood research. NARA Fort Worth holds Bureau of Indian Affairs Union Agency records. The Will Rogers Memorial Museum in Claremore has local history collections and the Rogers family papers. County Clerk records begin at statehood (1907). Oil production in the county generated lease and royalty records. The Oklahoma Historical Society holds Indian Territory newspapers and court records. FamilySearch has Oklahoma marriage and probate records. The Rogers County Genealogical Society has published cemetery transcriptions.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Tribal allotment records (Dawes Act) at NARA Fort Worth for pre-statehood land in Rogers County; County Clerk deed books from 1907

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Seminole County formed 1907 at statehood from the Seminole Nation, Indian Territory. County seat is Wewoka, former capital of the Seminole Nation. Major oil boom (Greater Seminole Oil Field, 1920s). All-Black towns and freedmen communities.
county_metadata.research_tips: Seminole County was part of the Seminole Nation in Indian Territory. The Seminole Nation was one of the Five Civilized Tribes removed to Indian Territory. Seminole tribal records (Dawes Rolls -- Seminole by Blood, Seminole Freedmen), allotment records, and tribal court records are at the Seminole Nation headquarters in Wewoka and NARA Fort Worth. The county experienced a massive oil boom in the late 1920s (Greater Seminole Oil Field), generating extensive oil lease, royalty, and guardianship records. The county had All-Black towns and significant freedmen communities. County Clerk records begin at statehood (1907). The Seminole Nation Museum in Wewoka has tribal and local history materials. The Oklahoma Historical Society holds Indian Territory newspapers and court records. FamilySearch has Oklahoma marriage and probate records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Tribal allotment records (Dawes Act) at NARA Fort Worth for pre-statehood land in Seminole County; County Clerk deed books from 1907

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Sequoyah County formed 1907 at statehood from the Cherokee Nation, Indian Territory. County seat is Sallisaw. Named for Sequoyah, inventor of the Cherokee syllabary. Sequoyah's cabin is a National Historic Landmark.
county_metadata.research_tips: Sequoyah County was part of the Cherokee Nation in Indian Territory. The county is named for Sequoyah (George Gist/Guess), inventor of the Cherokee syllabary. Cherokee Nation enrollment records, Dawes Rolls, and allotment records are critical. Sequoyah's cabin (a National Historic Landmark near Sallisaw) is a local heritage site. NARA Fort Worth holds Bureau of Indian Affairs Union Agency records. County Clerk records in Sallisaw begin at statehood (1907). Fort Smith, Arkansas (just across the border) was the seat of the US Court for the Western District of Arkansas, which had jurisdiction over Indian Territory criminal cases -- check NARA Fort Worth for those court records. The Oklahoma Historical Society holds Indian Territory newspapers. FamilySearch has Oklahoma marriage and probate records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Tribal allotment records (Dawes Act) at NARA Fort Worth for pre-statehood land in Sequoyah County; County Clerk deed books from 1907

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Indian Territory court records for the Sequoyah County area; US courts for Indian Territory (Northern, Central, Southern districts) handled criminal and civil cases; OHS holds court records, case files, and dockets; NARA Fort Worth has federal court records

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Stephens County formed 1907 at statehood from Chickasaw Nation and Comanche County. County seat is Duncan. Named for John H. Stephens. Major oil industry center. Halliburton founded here (1919).
county_metadata.research_tips: Stephens County was formed at statehood (1907) from portions of the Chickasaw Nation and Comanche County. Duncan, the county seat, became a major oil industry center. Chickasaw Nation tribal records (Dawes Rolls, allotment records) at NARA Fort Worth are relevant for the eastern portion. BLM GLO land patents are relevant for the western portion (formerly Comanche County/KCA lottery area). County Clerk records begin from 1907. Halliburton oil services company was founded in Duncan (1919), and company employment records may document workers. The Stephens County Historical Museum in Duncan has local materials. The Oklahoma Historical Society holds newspapers and court records. FamilySearch has Oklahoma marriage and probate records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Tribal allotment records (Dawes Act) at NARA Fort Worth for pre-statehood land in Stephens County; County Clerk deed books from 1907

source_profiles.source_name: Stephens County Deed Books / Chickasaw Nation Allotment Records -- County Clerk / NARA
source_profiles.repository: Stephens County Clerk, Duncan / NARA Fort Worth
source_profiles.coverage_notes: County deed books for Stephens County from 1907 (statehood); pre-statehood land was held as Chickasaw Nation allotments under the Dawes Act -- allotment records at NARA Fort Worth and OHS; County Clerk maintains deeds, mortgages, and liens
source_profiles.survival_notes: County records complete from 1907; allotment records at NARA are substantially complete

source_profiles.source_name: Stephens County Probate Records -- District Court
source_profiles.repository: Stephens County District Court, Duncan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, administrations, inventories, guardianships, and estate settlements for Stephens County; District Court has jurisdiction over probate in Oklahoma; guardianship records for minor allottees are particularly important in former Indian Territory counties

source_profiles.source_name: Dawes Rolls -- Chickasaw Nation Enrollment Records / NARA
source_profiles.repository: NARA Fort Worth / Chickasaw Nation / Oklahoma Historical Society
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Dawes Commission enrollment records for Chickasaw citizens (Chickasaw by Blood, Chickasaw Freedmen) in Stephens County area; includes application jackets with family information, census cards, and land allotment details; essential for tracing Chickasaw families before and after statehood

source_profiles.source_name: Stephens County Federal and Tribal Census Records
source_profiles.repository: NARA / FamilySearch / Ancestry / Oklahoma Historical Society
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census 1900-1950 for Stephens County; Chickasaw Nation census rolls and Indian Territory schedules at OHS and NARA

source_profiles.source_name: Stephens County Oil Lease and Corporation Records -- County Clerk / Corporation Commission
source_profiles.repository: Stephens County Clerk, Duncan / Oklahoma Corporation Commission, Oklahoma City
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Oil and gas lease records, royalty agreements, well permits, and corporation filings for Stephens County; County Clerk records oil/gas leases; Oklahoma Corporation Commission in Oklahoma City holds well records and production data; guardianship records for minor allottees with oil wealth are at the District Court

source_profiles.source_name: Stephens County Marriage Records -- County Clerk / OHS / FamilySearch
source_profiles.repository: Stephens County Clerk, Duncan / Oklahoma Historical Society
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage licenses and records for Stephens County from 1907; County Clerk issued marriage licenses in Oklahoma; OHS holds some county marriage records on microfilm; FamilySearch has indexed Oklahoma marriages; state vital records registration began 1908

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Indian Territory court records for the Stephens County area; US courts for Indian Territory (Northern, Central, Southern districts) handled criminal and civil cases; OHS holds court records, case files, and dockets; NARA Fort Worth has federal court records

county_metadata.parent_county: Beaver County, Oklahoma Territory (No Man's Land)
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Texas County formed 1907 at statehood from Beaver County (No Man's Land). County seat is Guymon. Oklahoma Panhandle. Severely impacted by the Dust Bowl.
county_metadata.research_tips: Texas County is in the Oklahoma Panhandle, formerly 'No Man's Land.' The county was created from Beaver County at statehood (1907). For pre-1907 records, check Beaver County. County Clerk records in Guymon begin from 1907. BLM GLO land patents are available online. The county was devastated by the Dust Bowl in the 1930s. Texas County later attracted Mexican and Central American agricultural workers -- immigration and naturalization records at the District Court may be valuable. The No Man's Land Museum in Goodwell has regional historical materials. The Oklahoma Historical Society holds territorial newspapers. Oklahoma Panhandle State University in Goodwell has local collections. FamilySearch has Oklahoma marriage records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO federal land patents for Texas County; County Clerk deed books from 1907

source_profiles.survival_notes: Records from 1907; GLO patents are complete for federal land entries
